coredata.dc:title: Stable superhydrophobic interfacial solar evaporator based on CuS@CDs hybrid photothermal composites towards ultrahigh-efficiency seawater evaporation

coredata.dc:description: Superhydrophobic evaporator driven by solar energy is very promising in seawater desalination. However, low evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency have greatly limited its practical applications. Here, we report a stable superhydrophobic solar evaporator with excellent photothermal performance for efficient saline evaporation. Specifically, CuS@carbon dots (CDs) composites are synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal approach. Then the composites are combined with fluorocarbon resin (FEVE) and sprayed on melamine sponge surface to create hierarchical structure to construct a superhydrophobic solar evaporator. Its excellent photothermal properties result from the synergistic effect of CuS and CDs in the composites. The temperature of the evaporator upper surface can rise to 125 °C within 3 min, and the evaporator presents high solar-vapor conversion efficiency (77.3%) and water evaporation rate (1.76 kg∙m−2∙h−1) under 1.5 Sun. Excitingly, for saline, the evaporation rate remains little changed (1.71 kg∙m−2∙h−1). Even after 10 test cycles (300 min), the evaporation rate of saline water is still able to reach 1.40 kg∙m−2∙h−1. Furthermore, the evaporator keeps hydrophobic property after being continuously immersed in high-concentration saline for 40 days, exhibiting excellent salt resistance. The prepared superhydrophobic evaporator with excellent photothermal performance has great potential in the fields of seawater desalination and sewage treatment.

head.ce:title: Stable superhydrophobic interfacial solar evaporator based on CuS@CDs hybrid photothermal composites towards ultrahigh-efficiency seawater evaporation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Superhydrophobic evaporator driven by solar energy is very promising in seawater desalination. However, low evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency have greatly limited its practical applications. Here, we report a stable superhydrophobic solar evaporator with excellent photothermal performance for efficient saline evaporation. Specifically, CuS@carbon dots (CDs) composites are synthesized by a facile one-step hydrothermal approach. Then the composites are combined with fluorocarbon resin (FEVE) and sprayed on melamine sponge surface to create hierarchical structure to construct a superhydrophobic solar evaporator. Its excellent photothermal properties result from the synergistic effect of CuS and CDs in the composites. The temperature of the evaporator upper surface can rise to 125 °C within 3 min, and the evaporator presents high solar-vapor conversion efficiency (77.3%) and water evaporation rate (1.76 kg∙m−2∙h−1) under 1.5 Sun. Excitingly, for saline, the evaporation rate remains little changed (1.71 kg∙m−2∙h−1). Even after 10 test cycles (300 min), the evaporation rate of saline water is still able to reach 1.40 kg∙m−2∙h−1. Furthermore, the evaporator keeps hydrophobic property after being continuously immersed in high-concentration saline for 40 days, exhibiting excellent salt resistance. The prepared superhydrophobic evaporator with excellent photothermal performance has great potential in the fields of seawater desalination and sewage treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: To meet the challenges of demands in clean water and pursue to utilization of new energy [1–5]. Rational application of solar energy, which is clean and renewable, can effectively alleviate the energy shortage [6–9]. Researchers have turned their attention to accessing freshwater by solar-driven seawater evaporators, for most of conventional desalination methods are energy-intensive and require expensive equipment, which severely limits the promotion of seawater desalination technology [10–15].Traditional solar-driven evaporators rely on photothermal materials placed at the bottom or in an intermediate carrier fluid to absorb or transfer heat directly to the bulk liquid, both of which suffer from high heat loss [16]. Compared with traditional models, interfacial evaporators can effectively confine and utilize heat on the water surface, minimizing thermal diffusion in other directions [17]. This feature enhances the efficiency of solar energy utilization. The current focus is on continuous optimization for the evaporation performance of interfacial solar evaporators. And many excellent interfacial solar evaporators have been brought out [18–20].

Whereas, most interfacial solar evaporators are faced with a problem that because of the rapid water evaporation on its upper surface, it is difficult for the solute to diffuse back into the bottom solution in time, and deposit on the upper surface or the sponge skeleton inside of the evaporator to form salt scales blocking pores [1,21–23], which significantly decreases the water evaporation rate of the solar evaporator. The hierarchic rough superhydrophobic surface structure can decrease the contact area between the liquid and solid, thereby reducing the crystallization area of salts at the water-solid interface [24,25]. Shi et al. fabricated a Janus evaporator using a superhydrophobic surface to realize extreme high salt-resistance [26]. Hu et al. designed a solar-driven interfacial evaporation with a yolk@shell superhydrophobic/superhydrophilic structure for stable desalination [1]. Guo et al. fabricated a salt-blocking Janus evaporator successfully with a wettability gradient containing superhydrophobic porous layers [27]. According to previous designs of superhydrophobic solar evaporator, only superhydrophobic surface cannot transport water to the surface.

In order to achieve high water evaporation rate without salt blocking, the solar evaporators can be realized using a suitable substrate combining an excellent superhydrophobic surface. Because of its three-dimensional porous hydrophilic network structure, melamine sponge can transport water quickly. Moreover, it also has the advantages of low density [13], low thermal conductivity [28,29], low cost[30] and commercial availability [31,32], thus it has been used as substrate of solar evaporator in many researchers' work [33–35]. Fan et al [34]. immersed melamine sponge in a pre-configured reduced graphene oxide (rGO) colloid solution, obtaining a light-absorbing coating on its upper surface by the dip coating method. Then, an aluminum foil was put at the bottom of sponge as a reflective layer to enhance light absorption. The resulting solar evaporator has a high saline (3.5 wt% NaCl) evaporation rate with 1.496 kg m−2∙h−1 under 1 Sun. In addition, good photothermal performance is crucial for solar evaporator. There are many materials commonly used to achieve photothermal performance, such as graphene [33,36], carbon nanotubes [37,38], and carbon dots (CDs) [39,40]. CDs have the advantages of broad-spectrum light absorption from 200 nm to 800 nm, convenient production, high photothermal conversion efficiency, and abundant raw materials [39,41]. It is time to bring out an improvement strategy for fabricating a solar-driven evaporators based on photothermal superhydrophobic coating and melamine sponge substrate to maximize performance and improve reliability.

Herein, we prepared CuS@CDs composites with synergistically photothermal properties by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The CuS@CDs composites can be combined with fluorocarbon resin (FEVE) to create a hierarchical structure on the melamine sponge surface by simple spraying method, resulting in forming a photothermal superhydrophobic coating. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of CuS and solid CDs, the resulting evaporator has excellent photothermal conversion efficiency, which can continuously transport water upwards and quickly evaporate to achieve the effect of desalinating seawater. The solar evaporator surface temperature can rise to 125 °C within 3 min under 1.5 Sun, showing high solar-vapor conversion efficiency (77.27%) and evaporation rate (1.76 kg∙m−2∙h−1). Recently, Wang et al [42]. fabricated a hybrid photothermal membrane constructed by carbon and porous TiO2 layers with an evaporation rate of 1.42 kg∙m−2∙h−1 and photothermal conversion efficiency of 59.43% under 1.5 Sun irradiation. Chang et al [43]. reported a three-dimensional porous solar evaporator with a photothermal conversion efficiency of about 60% under 1.5 Sun irradiation. Compared to these reported results, our prepared evaporator obviously has a higher evaporative solar-to-steam conversion efficiency. The prepared evaporator is simple in preparation method, low in cost, excellent in performance and convenient in use, which can realize large-scale and industrialized production, showing great application potential in seawater desalination or sewage treatment.

Sulfourea, glucose and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were provided by Sigma-Aldrich. FeCl3∙6H2O, CuSO4∙5H2O, NaOH, NaCl, ethyl acetate (EA), and absolute ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. fluorocarbon resin (FEVE, solid content ≥50%) was bought from Shanghai Fukang New Material Technology co., Ltd. Melamine sponge was purchased from the local supermarket, which was ultrasonically cleaned and dried before use.

Preparation of Cu2O[44]: firstly, 1.25 g CuSO4∙5H2O was put in 50 mL water by stirring of 300 rpm, then the solution temperature was kept at 55 °C for 2 min. Next, 30 mL of 3 M NaOH was quickly put into the above solution, and blue Cu(OH)2 was quickly produced in the solution. Then the temperature was adjusted to 70 °C, and the blue color quickly changed into dark blue. 5 min later, 0.3 g glucose was then put to the mixed solution, which was kept at 70 °C for 17 min. The suspension color slowly changed into red, revealing the formation of Cu2O. The resulting red precipitate was obtained by centrifugation (9000 rpm 5 min), which was washed three times with ethanol and water respectively, and the dried Cu2O was sealed and stored.

Preparation of CuS@CDs: 0.032 g of the above Cu2O, 0.108 g of OPD, 0.026 g of thiourea, and 62.5 μL FeCl3∙6H2O (0.5 M) were successively added to 10 mL water and stirred rapidly at 90 °C for 6 h. Then the resulting solution was then centrifuged three times with ethanol (5000 rpm 5 min) and dried in an oven at 60 °C to synthesize CuS@CDs composites by one-step method, denoted as CuS@CDs.

Preparation of CuS by modification of the reported method[44]: CuS microncage was synthesized by using the sulfidation reaction of Cu2O. Specifically, 0.032 g Cu2O and 0.026 g thiourea were directly added to 10 mL water in sequence, and the mixture solution was quickly stirred at 90 °C for 6 h. Finally, the obtained product was rinsed three times by ethanol centrifugation (9000 rpm 5 min).

Preparation of yellow CDs[45]: 0.108 g OPD and 62.5 μL FeCl3∙6H2O (0.5 M) were successively added to 10 mL deionized water, and then were rapidly stirred at 90 °C for 6 h. Finally, the solid state CDs were achieved by evaporating to dryness using a rotary evaporator.

The fabrication process of solar evaporator is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Firstly, a certain mass (0.03, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 g) of FEVE and certain mass of CuS@CDs composites (0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04, and 0.05 g) were put into 10 mL EA. The melamine sponge was cut into small pieces (2.5 × 2.5 × 0.5 cm3), ultrasonically cleaned, and then dried. The configured solution was then sprayed onto the dried sponge surface. The distance between the spray gun and the sprayed surface of the sponge was about 15 cm, and the spray gun moved at a constant speed to make the coating uniform. Lastly, the coated sponge was cured at 80 °C for 2 h in an oven, and the resulting solar evaporator was named CuS@C-FEVE. Only upper side of this solar evaporator is coated, that is, only one side has superhydrophobic performance, and the other sides are superhydrophilic. In addition, solar evaporators with only CuS (or only CDs) were also conducted as the control experiments. That is, solid CDs or CuS was used to prepare solar evaporator containing CDs only (named C-FEVE) and photothermal evaporator containing CuS only (named CuS-FEVE) using the same procedure as CuS@C-FEVE evaporator.

The specific operation of the self-cleaning ability test of CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator is as follows: the solar evaporator with the best performance was glued to the glass slide which was tilted with a small angle (∼15°). Then some fine sand was scattered on it to simulate pollutants, and water droplets were continuously dropped on the solar evaporator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance and water evaporation capacity test
ce:section.ce:para: A 300 W xenon lamp with a 1.5G atmosphere spectrum filter was used to simulate sunlight. During the test, the upper surface of the evaporator was vertically irradiated under the simulated sunlight with a distance of 50 cm, and the indoor temperature was 27 ± 2 °C. Light intensity (1.5 Sun, 2.7 Sun, 3.4 Sun) was controlled by adjusting the current (10 A, 15 A, 20 A), and the simulated light intensity was characterized by a full-spectrum optical power meter. Besides, the upper surface temperature of the evaporator samples under light was tested with a handheld infrared thermometer. Moreover, the superhydrophobic solar evaporator was placed in a petri dish (35 mm in diameter) containing with water (or 3.5 wt% NaCl solution) to simulate the photothermal effect and water (brine) evaporation capacity of the superhydrophobic evaporator under different intensities of light. Then, the petri dish was filled with the same amount of water to make the same water level for each test and dish inner diameter was only slightly larger than that of the evaporator to decrease heat loss during evaporation.

A series of stability experiments (including water impact test, latex gloves and sandpaper friction test, high temperature test, and salt resistance test) were conducted to evaluate the durability of the superhydrophobic photothermal evaporator. For the water impact resistance test, we used a washing bottle to shoot water in a direction of 45° from the superhydrophobic coating surface; For the friction resistance experiment, the ordinary finger friction experiment was firstly adopted, that is, the coated side of the superhydrophobic solar evaporator was repeatedly pressed and rubbed on the hand with latex gloves; Next, the ability of the evaporator to resist sandpaper friction was also tested by placing the coated surface of the evaporator on 1000 mesh sandpaper and applying a 100 g weight on top of it. During the friction test, the evaporator was pushed vertically from the side to advance, with a distance of 10 cm each time. Besides, the test was performed at a new position every two times, and the water contact angles (WCAs) of the sample were tested every 20 times of friction. For the high temperature resistance test, the samples were placed at different temperatures for 2 h and then WCAs was tested. For the salt resistance test, the samples were put in salt water with high concentration (10 wt% NaCl) for 40 days of continuous immersion, during which the petri dish was sealed and kept in the dark. Then the sample was taken out and cleaned with water and ethanol respectively, and the WCAs of the sample before and after soaking were both tested.

The morphology of the composites and the coating surface were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-7500F). The elemental composition and content of the coating on the surface of the solar evaporator were tested by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, Hitachi S-4800). The morphology and size of CDs in the composites were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F). The chemical composition of the original sponge and the solar evaporator surface were determined by the attenuated total reflectance fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR, Nicolet 6700). The crystal phase and diffraction peaks of the prepared samples were investigated by (XRD, D8 DISCOVER). WCA was determined by a Drop Shape Analyzer (DSA100S). Specifically, the average value of WCAs was measured with 5 μL water droplets in three different locations of the sample surface. The absorption spectra of the original sponge and the photothermal evaporator in the solar spectral wavelength range (200–2500 nm) were tested by UV2600 UV–vis spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical composition of the prepared materials and solar evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: For the composites, after calculation, the content of Cu element (36.14 wt%) is higher than that of S element (11.52 wt%), indicating that there are other compounds containing Cu besides CuS (Fig. S1a, b). Combined with the content of O element (6.92 wt%), it is obvious that there is template particle Cu2O in the composites. XRD analysis was characterized on the fabricated materials to verify the chemical composition of the synthesized materials. The XRD spectra of the obtained red particles are consistent with the XRD spectrum of cuprite, confirming that the obtained particles are Cu2O (Fig. S1c). For the composites, the XRD spectrum not only shows the presence of CuS (covellite) (Fig. 2a), but also observes the diffraction peaks of the unconsumed template particle Cu2O and CuO produced. In addition, the prepared solid CDs and composites were evenly dispersed in ethanol, respectively. It is found that both of them can show yellow under excitation of 395 nm UV light (the inset in Fig. 2a). The ATR-FTIR of the solar evaporator prepared with the composites is presented in Fig. 2b. Compared to the pristine sponge, the photothermal coating obviously appears new peaks at 1211 and 943 cm−1, which are the vibration peak caused by CF2 and CF3 [46,47]. Besides, vibration peaks caused by CH2 and CH3 appears at 2916 and 2848 cm−1 [48,49], which indicates that the coating contains a large number of silane chains. These results all indicate that FEVE is successfully introduced into the photothermal coating. The vibration peak caused by CF2 and CF3 and the vibration peaks caused by CH2 and CH3 can be also seen in the FTIR spectra of the only CuS and only CDs solar evaporators (Fig. S2a). The element mapping of superhydrophobic CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator can be seen in Fig. 2c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology of the prepared particles and solar evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: By measuring the SEM of Cu2O synthesized in the experiment, it can be seen that it presents an octahedral structure with a particle size of ∼2.66 μm (Fig. S3a1-a3). After the sulfidation reaction, part of template Cu2O particles were consumed, and the CuS obtained successfully transcribed the form of template Cu2O particles with a polyhedral structure (Fig. S3b1-b3). Besides, the edge length of CuS particles is about 8.34 μm. From the high-magnification SEM image of the particle surface, we can see that the CuS polyhedral particle is composed of small-sized nanoparticles (Fig. S3b3). When the CuS@CDs composites are synthesized by the one-step method, the size of the particle is similar to that of the CuS synthesize, which is also about 8.34 μm (Fig. S3c1-c3). However, its polyhedral structure is no longer obvious, similar to spherical, and there are many agglomerated particles on its surface, which may be due to the oxidative polymerization of OPD during the synthesis of CuS, resulting in the agglomeration of small particles on the surface of CuS.

CuS@C-FEVE evaporators prepared with different contents of composites also was performed by the SEM tests. It can be seen from Fig. 3a1-a3 that the original melamine sponge has a structure of three-dimensional cross-linked porous network, the surface of the skeleton is very smooth, and the average pore size is 86–170 μm.Such a macroporous network structure can enable water transport rapidly. In order to make the sponge float on the surface of water and consecutively pump water upwards, its surface is sprayed with CuS@C-FEVE coating, and the effect of different contents of composites on the morphology of the coating was also investigated. With the increase of composites content, the surface of sponge skeleton becomes coarser and coarser, the diameter becomes thicker, and the pore size decreases slightly. However, the upper surface morphology of the evaporator is still similar to that of original melamine sponge, and the three-dimensional cross-linked porous network structure is well preserved. When the amount of the composites is 0.01 g (Fig. 3b1-b3), there are relatively too much FEVE, the composites are completely covered on the sponge skeleton, thus the appropriate roughness cannot be constructed and the surface is still relatively smooth; When the content of the composites increases to 0.03 g (Fig. 3c1-c3), the surface of the coating presents a papillary structure, and the composites can be glued together by the resin, so it would not be too loose. Therefore, a coating with appropriate roughness can be constructed on the sponge skeleton; When the composites are 0.05 g (Fig. 3d1-d3), the sponge skeleton becomes very rough, the resin is not enough to coat the composites, and the composites can only be loosely distributed on the sponge skeleton, which is not conducive to the mechanical stability of the coating. In addition, by observing TEM images of the composites (Fig. S4a, b), it can be found that there are many point-like materials with a spacing of ∼0.2 nm and a diameter of ∼3.45 nm (Fig. S4c), which represents the (100) in-plane lattice fringes of graphite. It is further proved that the composites contain a large number of yellow CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface wettability of the solar evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: In order to keep the solar evaporator fabricated floating stably on the surface of water, the sun-facing side was designed to have excellent superhydrophobic performance. Therefore, it is necessary to explore the factors affecting the wettability of the photothermal evaporator upper surface. Fig. 4a shows the effect of different contents of FEVE on the WCA of the CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator upper surface. It can be seen from the Fig. 4a that with the increase of FEVE content, WCA of evaporator gradually decreases. We suspect that FEVE will be wrapped too much, reducing the coating surface roughness, though it contains C-F groups with low surface energy. With the increase of composites content, the hydrophobic performance of the coating on the upper surface of the evaporator first improves and then decreases (Fig. 4b), which is because too much or too little composites make the surface roughness of the coating too large or too small. This is also illustrated by the SEM images of the coatings prepared by different contents of the composites shown in Fig. 3 . In addition, the color of the evaporator upper surface gradually becomes darker, from white to black (Fig. 4c). Only when the content of FEVE is 0.03 g and the composites are 0.03 g, the evaporator can achieve the best superhydrophobic performance (160°). At this point, because of the low surface energy properties of FEVE and the rough structure formed by it and composites, the upper surface of CuS@C-FEVE evaporator has excellent superhydrophobicity, and the water droplets are stable spherical on its surface (Fig. 4d), while the other sides of the evaporator are hydrophilic.

The double-layer superhydrophobic/superhydrophilic structure of CuS@C-FEVE evaporator can make it float on the water surface and pump water upwards continuously, which is conductive to the efficient use of solar energy to make water evaporate quickly [45,50,51]. In addition, some comparative experiments were also conducted. When only 0.03 g CuS or 0.03 g solid CDs are used to fabricate the photothermal coating, the WCAs can only reach 140.2° and 124.5°, respectively (Fig. 4e). This is because the addition of composites can make the system contain both micro- and nano-scale materials, which can form hierarchical rough structures on the surface of sponge skeleton to construct suitable surfaces.

The roughness factor values a lot on a superhydrophobic surface. In reality, a completely smooth surface does not exist. Most surfaces will always have a more or less rough structure. The effect of different contents of FEVE and composite on WCA comes from different surface roughness. The relationship between surface roughness and WCA can be explained by Wenzel-model, as shown in eq. (1):
(1)
cos
θ w
= r cos θ
where θw is the apparent WCA of the droplet on a rough surface, θ is the WCA of a droplet on a completely smooth solid surface, and r is the roughness factor, which refers to the ratio of the actual solid-liquid contact area to the projected surface area. We presume that the purpose of adjusting the content can meet the maximum roughness factor in this work. And different contents of FEVE and composite may contribute to different values of r, which decrease when the addition of FEVE increases from 0.03 g to 0.3 g, but are highest when the composite is 0.03 g in Fig. 4a.

However, the coatings containing CuS or CDs alone cannot construct suitable roughness. When FEVE is not added, the coating sprayed on the upper surface of the evaporator will be slowly infiltrated by water (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-cleaning and light absorption capability test of CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: When solar evaporators are applied to the desalination of seawater, it is inevitable to encounter pollutants such as sediment. Therefore, it is necessary to evaluate the self-cleaning ability of the evaporator upper surface. As shown in Fig. 5a1-a3, Video S1, the solar evaporator is placed with a tilted angle of 15°, and then scattered some fine sand on their upper surfaces to simulate pollutants. Then water droplets continuously are dripped on the upper surface with a rubber dropper. It can be seen that water droplets are able to roll off rapidly and take away the pollutants on the upper surface without infiltrating the coating of the upper surface, which is attributed to the excellent superhydrophobic properties of the photothermal coating.

The absorption spectra of the solar evaporators with only CuS and CDs in the wavelength range of the solar spectrum (200–2500 nm) are shown in Fig. S2b. The solar evaporator with bare CuS can absorb the light across the UV–Vis-NIR regions, while the addition of CDs can enhance the absorbance of the CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator from 250 to 500 nm. In addition, the solar absorption capacity of CuS@C-FEVE evaporator was also tested. Its absorption spectrum in the wavelength range of solar spectrum (200–2500 nm) is shown in Fig. 5b. The absorbance of the solar evaporator is about 65% in the range of 200–1870 nm, and about 53.5% in the range of 1870–2500 nm. However, the absorbance of the original sponge at 200–2500 nm is only 2.6%, indicating that the CuS@C-FEVE coating can dramatically improve the sunlight absorption capacity of the evaporator. This is attributed to the high absorbance of CuS and CDs materials in the photothermal coatings and the rough CuS@C-FEVE coating with large surface area. Moreover, the porous structure of CuS@C-FEVE evaporator can greatly improve its absorbance through multiple light absorption, increase light scattering and reduce light reflection [52,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance and evaporation capacity of solar evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the good absorption capacity of sunlight, the prepared CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator has excellent photothermal performance. Moreover, during the preparation of the evaporator, the content of the CuS@CDs composites in the CuS@C-FEVE suspension has an obvious effect on the photothermal performance of the CuS@C-FEVE. As shown in Fig. 6a, the temperature of the upper surface of the evaporator rises from room temperature to the highest temperature within 3 min under 1.5 Sun, and the temperature remains basically unchanged after continuous irradiation for 25 min. After the sunlight is turned off, the surface temperature gradually returns to room temperature within 5 min. However, there are some differences in the photothermal properties of the solar evaporator with different contents of CuS@CDs. For the original melamine sponge, the upper surface temperature can only reach a maximum of 40 °C. However, the temperature of the evaporator with the introduction of the superhydrophobic photothermal coating is significantly increased, and the photothermal performance is improved with the increase of the composites content. When the CuS@CDs composites are 0.01 g, the upper surface temperature of the evaporator can reach up to 102 °C. At 0.02 g and 0.03 g, the maximum temperature of the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator upper surface can reach 119 °C and 125 °C, respectively. Whereas, when the composites contents are 0.04 g and 0.05 g, the maximum temperature of solar evaporator are 122 °C and 125 °C, respectively, which is not much different from the maximum temperature at 0.03 g. This shows that with the further increase of the content of the CuS@CDs composites, the photothermal performance of the CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator reaches its peak value, which is almost unchanged and cannot be further improved. Furthermore, the radiant intensity of sunlight also has a huge effect on the evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency of the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator. Fig. 6b demonstrates the photothermal performance of the CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator (0.03 g composites) under different light intensities. As shown in the figure, the upper surface temperature of the evaporator also rises to the highest temperature within 3 min, and with the light intensity increasing from 1.5 Sun to 2.7 Sun and 3.4 Sun, the upper surface temperature increases from 125 °C to 136 °C and 158 °C. After continuous irradiation for 20 min, the temperature remains unchanged, and after removing the sunlight, the temperature gradually drops to room temperature within 5 min. Besides, the inset parts show how the upper surface temperature of the evaporator was tested by the infrared thermometer under 1.5 Sun irradiation for different times. The above test results all mean that the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator can rapidly convert the sunlight absorbed into heat. Moreover, the photothermal performance of evaporators prepared by CuS and solid CDs only are also tested as a comparison. As shown in Fig. 6c, when the solar evaporator prepared only by CuS is irradiated by sunlight of different intensities (1.5 Sun, 2.7 Sun, 3.4 Sun), its upper surface temperature can only reach 103 °C, 125 °C and 135 °C, respectively. For the solar evaporator prepared with only CDs (Fig. 6d), the upper surface temperature can only reach 89 °C, 96 °C and 102 °C, respectively. Comparing them with the solar evaporators prepared from the composites, the photothermal performances are both poor, which also indicates that the excellent photothermal performance of the evaporators is caused by the synergistic effect of CuS and CDs in the composites.

Next, the water evaporation rate of the solar evaporator is tested and the effect of different light intensities is explored on the photothermal conversion capacity of the evaporator. The solar-vapor conversion efficiency ( η ) is calculated based on formula (2)[54,55]:
(2)
η =
m ̇
h LV
q i
C opt
where
m ̇
represents the evaporation rate (minus the natural evaporation rate under dark conditions),
C opt
is the sunlight number,
q i
is the intensity of one sunlight (1 kW∙m−2), and
h LV
is the total enthalpy of a phase change including sensible heat and phase change enthalpy.
h LV
= λ + C ∆ T , where λ is the latent heat of phase transition, C is the specific heat capacity of water, and ∆ T is the temperature change during evaporation.

As shown in Fig. S6a-c, the original sponge sinks at the bottom when placed in the petri dish, while the solar evaporator can float on the surface of water because of its excellent superhydrophobic performance. Then xenon lamp is used to simulate the vertical irradiation of sunlight on the CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator upper surface (50 cm) to test its evaporation performance. As can be seen from Fig. 7a, with the light intensity increasing from 1.5 Sun to 2.7 Sun to 3.4 Sun, the water evaporation rate of the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator with the best performance also increases gradually (from 1.76 kg∙m−2∙h−1 to 2.33 kg∙m−2∙h−1 to 2.61 kg∙m−2∙h−1). Even under 1.5 Sun irradiation, the evaporator still has high water evaporation rate and solar-vapor conversion efficiency, which ensures the rapid evaporation of water. Nevertheless, although the increase of light intensity enhances the water evaporation rate, the solar-vapor conversion efficiency gradually decreases (from 77.30% to 65.89% to 58.01%). Besides, when only CuS or solid CDs with the same content are used to prepare the solar evaporator, their water evaporation rate and η have the same trend as the above CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator (Fig. 7b, c). Specifically, for the CuS-FEVE evaporator, under different light intensities, the water evaporation rate is 1.48–1.88 kg∙m−2∙h−1, and the η is only 63.88% ∼ 36.84%. For the C-FEVE evaporator, the water evaporation rate is 1.16–1.40 kg∙m−2∙h−1, and η is only 50.41% ∼ 26.68%. When the original sponge is used as the evaporator, its water evaporation rate (0.36–0.45 kg∙m−2∙h−1) and η (7.09% ∼ 6.75%) are much lower than those of the CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator. Furthermore, even if the light intensity is enhanced, the evaporation rate and η does not change much (Fig. 7d), which further indicates that both CuS and solid-state CDs have light absorption ability and photothermal conversion performance. Whereas, the CuS@CDs composites prepared by one-step method have better performance than the two materials alone. From this, CuS and solid CDs in the composites have synergistic effect, which can improve the light absorption capacity and photothermal conversion performance of CuS@C-FEVE evaporator. Evaporation rate and evaporation efficiency of the evaporators in this work were shown in Table S1.

In addition, the cycling stability of the evaporator is also tested. In the ten test cycles, each cycle lasts to light for 30 min, the water evaporation rate and η change little and are relatively stable, maintaining at 1.76–1.4 kg∙m−2∙h−1 and 77.27% ∼ 60.86% (Fig. 7e). Moreover, to further simulate the seawater environment, the same test was performed by placing the evaporator into a petri dish containing a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. After ten-cycle test, the saline evaporation rate of this evaporator is 1.71–1.4 kg∙m−2∙h−1, and the η is 75.01% ∼ 59.98% (Fig. 7f). Although the evaporation ability of the evaporator in saline is slightly weaker than that in deionized water, its evaporation rate and solar-to-vapor conversion efficiency are still relatively steady in ten test cycles, indicating that the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator has promising application potential in seawater purification. Recently, Wu et al [44]. tested the resilience of a cotton-CuS-agarose aerogel subjected to continuous 1.0 Sun illumination over 15 successive cycles. Their results underscored the material's durability, maintaining a consistent evaporation rate between 1.4 and 1.5 kg m−2 h−1. Zhao et al [56]. carried out a series of 10 cycles to ascertain the steady operation of the Co–CN–WA evaporator. The evaporation rate is consistently between 1.2 and 1.4 kg m−2 h−1. The good salt resistance and cycling stability of the evaporator can be explained as follows: in the solar evaporation process, the salt concentration in the evaporator gradually increases from bottom to top, often forming salt precipitation on the evaporator upper surface. Due to the superhydrophilicity and macroporous network structure of the melamine sponge, this evaporator is able to quickly replenish the saline solution through the capillary effect. As a result, the salt concentration gradient from bottom to top in the evaporator is significantly reduced, and the salt does not precipitate to block the transport channels of the evaporator. Therefore, the evaporation rate of solar evaporator is still relatively stable in the process of saline evaporation under long-term sunlight irradiation.

Solar evaporators will inevitably encounter various harsh environments during its use, so the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator prepared in the experiment was conducted a series of stability tests: water impact test, high temperature and friction resistance test. As shown in Fig. 8a, Video S2, a washing bottle is used to shoot the water flow in a direction of 45° from the superhydrophobic photothermal coating on the upper surface of the evaporator. It can be found that the water flow bounces off the surface in the opposite direction, without any infiltration and diffusing to the coating surface. Even after continuous impact for 30 min, the WCA of the coating is still as high as 155°. Since the evaporator in the working state is always in a high temperature environment, high temperature resistance test is important. Considering that the working temperature of the CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator is generally around 125 °C, it is placed at 100–200 °C for 2 h in the experiment to measure its high temperature resistance. As illustrated in Fig. 8b, after the evaporator is placed at different temperatures for 2 h, its WCA decreases slightly with the increase of temperature, and remains above 150°.

In addition, the sandpaper friction resistance test was also performed on the photothermal coating of evaporator. According to Fig. 8c, the superhydrophobic photothermal coating layer of the evaporator is directly facing 1000 mesh sandpaper, and a 100 g weight is placed on top of the evaporator and the evaporator is pushed vertically by hand from its side. As shown in Fig. 8d, with the increase of friction times, the WCA of the evaporator first decreases and then increases to 168°. This is because before the number of friction times is 60, with the increase of the number of friction times, the photothermal coating on the surface of the evaporator is gradually worn away, and many composites are worn off or even the coating layer is smoothed, resulting in a gradual decrease in roughness and hydrophobic performance. When the sandpaper friction test is continued (100 times), the residual resin on the sponge skeleton surface is continuously worn away, resulting in cracks and burrs (Fig. 9 a1-a2), which instead restructures the surface roughness and make it increase, thereby improving the hydrophobic performance. In contrast, the surface of the CuS@C solar evaporator prepared without FEVE is rough but loose (Fig. 9b1-b2). Even after only 20 times of friction on sandpaper, most of the composites on the sponge skeleton surface are worn away and the skeleton is exposed (Fig. 9c1-c2). Hence, the friction resistance performance is much lower than that of the solar evaporator prepared with FEVE. It can also be detected from Fig. 10 a1-a3,10b1-b3 that for the CuS@C solar evaporator, when it is rubbed back and forth on the palm of the latex gloved hand once, and the composites (black part) will fall off on the latex glove. In contrast, the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator does not leave traces on the gloves after repeated friction. These results all indicate that the addition of FEVE significantly improves the mechanical stability of the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator.

To investigate the adaptability of the CuS@C-FEVE evaporator under harsh conditions, its salt tolerance stability was investigated. Fig. 10c1 displays the optical photograph of the CuS@C-FEVE solar evaporator immersed in 10 wt% NaCl solution, and the evaporator is able to float on the surface of water. After soaked in high-concentration saline for 40 days, the water in NaCl solution was partially evaporated, but the evaporator remained unchanged and could still float on the NaCl solution as shown in Fig. 10c2. Moreover, the WCA of the evaporator can still reach 143° with highly hydrophobic, which means that the evaporator may have the resistance to saline corrosion and may have a chance to be applied to seawater desalination for a long time to some extent. This excellent stability is mainly due to the natural high stability of melamine sponge and FEVE, and FEVE can combine CuS@CDs composites with melamine sponge well.

In summary, we synthesized octahedral Cu2O and used it as the template to prepare CuS@CDs composites by one-step hydrothermal method. Then FEVE was used as low surface energy modifier and binder to obtain superhydrophobic photothermal coating on the surface of melamine sponge by simple spraying method. Finally, solar evaporator with excellent performance was prepared. This evaporator's excellent photothermal performance results from the synergistic effect of CuS and solid CDs in composites as well as its own porous network structure, which enables it not only to float stably on water surface but also to rapidly transport water from bottom to top. The surface temperature of the evaporator can rise to 125 °C within 3 min under 1.5 Sun, and the solar-vapor conversion efficiency and evaporation rate can reach 77.3% and 1.76 kg∙m−2∙h−1, respectively. Even if the coated surface of the evaporator was rubbed 100 times (1000 cm) on the 1000 mesh sandpaper under the load of 100 g, subjected to continuous water impact for 30 min or immersed in high-concentration saline for 40 days, it did not lose its hydrophobic properties, showing excellent stability. Therefore, the stable superhydrophobic photothermal evaporator fabricated by the facile method has great application prospect in seawater desalination, which greatly broadens the application range of superhydrophobic coatings.
